medications.indication_rationale: Extensive, multi-lesion, rapidly spreading facial non-bullous impetigo warrants a systemic anti-staphylococcal/streptococcal antibiotic rather than topical therapy alone. In telehealth the AI Doctor does not prescribe autonomously — it arranges a prompt prescriber/in-person review; any dose statement here is the source clinician's plan and must be confirmed by the prescriber against a current formulary.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 500 mg orally, four times daily, for 7 days (adult; source-note plan)
contraindications_in_this_case: Immediate (anaphylactic) or delayed hypersensitivity to penicillins — patient reports no known allergy, so not contraindicated here; confirm before prescribing.

medications.indication_rationale: A reasonable oral alternative for widespread impetigo, and the option of choice if there is non-severe penicillin hypersensitivity; covers the usual staphylococcal/streptococcal organisms.
medications.dose_route_frequency: dose to be set by the prescriber against a current formulary — not specified in the source note, so not stated here

medications.indication_rationale: Topical mupirocin is first-line for LOCALISED impetigo, but is insufficient as sole therapy for this extensive, rapidly spreading multi-lesion facial disease, where a systemic antibiotic is required.

Must be STOPPED, not continued. Applying a topical corticosteroid to an untreated bacterial skin infection locally suppresses immunity and accelerates spread — continuing or recommending it is an error of commission.
medications.deprescribing_note: Advise immediate cessation of all topical corticosteroid on the affected skin; it has been aggravating the infection.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Stop the over-the-counter hydrocortisone cream immediately and complete the full antibiotic course even once the sores look better.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The steroid was accelerating the infection; stopping it and finishing the antibiotic prevents relapse and resistance.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Twice daily, soak a clean cloth in warm soapy water and gently press on the sores for about 5 minutes to soften and lift the crusts, then wipe them away; wash hands before and after.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Removing crusts lets the antibiotic and the immune system reach the bacteria; the source clinician's care plan. Leaving thick crusts intact shields the bacteria.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Strict infection control: use a separate towel and pillowcase, wash all towels, pillowcases and bed linen in hot water now, do not share personal items, and wash hands rigorously after touching the sores. Consider staying home from work/school until 24 hours of effective antibiotics.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Impetigo is highly contagious via exudate and shared items; the patient lives with family and shares towels, so household transmission risk is high.

patient_education_points.point: This looks like a common, highly contagious superficial bacterial skin infection. Because it is extensive and spreading on the face, it usually needs oral antibiotics rather than a cream alone — arranged through a prompt in-person or prescriber assessment.

You are considered contagious until you have had about 24 hours of effective antibiotics. Wash all towels, pillowcases and sheets in hot water now, don't share personal items, and wash your hands well after touching the sores.
Stop using the hydrocortisone cream — steroid creams make this type of infection spread faster.
With effective antibiotics the spreading should settle within about 48 hours and the skin usually heals in 7-10 days without scarring, as long as the crusts aren't deeply picked or scratched.

SMS-style summary: School sores (impetigo). Very contagious. Needs antibiotics. Wash all towels and linen in hot water now; stop steroid cream; see a doctor promptly and go to ED if the redness becomes hot and painful or an eye swells.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: that the lesions are drying and not spreading, no new lesions, no developing cellulitis or systemic features, and that the antibiotic is being tolerated; review swab result if taken.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: spreading despite 48 hours of antibiotics, new or increasing pain, deep/hot redness, fever or feeling unwell, or any spread toward the eye.

must_include_items: Recognise a spreading facial bacterial skin infection from the honey-crusted lesions and history (without needing to name the exact organism)
Arrange a prompt same-day / next-day in-person or prescriber assessment for oral antibiotics rather than managing blind over chat
Explicitly advise STOPPING the over-the-counter hydrocortisone cream
Give infection-control advice: don't share towels/linen, wash them in hot water, hand hygiene, contagious until ~24h of antibiotics
Safety-net for deep/hot/painful redness, fever/rigors, and any eye involvement
Keep eczema herpeticum / HSV in mind as the reason the lesions should be seen in person
acceptable_alternatives: Recommending the patient's usual GP or an urgent care clinic same-day is equivalent to arranging a telehealth prescriber, as long as a prompt look at the lesions occurs.
Advising a bacterial swab is acceptable but must not delay starting antibiotics.
errors_of_omission: Failing to arrange any prescriber/in-person pathway for a spreading facial infection
Failing to advise stopping the topical corticosteroid
Failing to give infection-control / contagiousness advice
Failing to safety-net for eye involvement or developing cellulitis
errors_of_commission: Recommending or continuing the topical corticosteroid
Recommending topical antibiotic alone as adequate for extensive facial disease
Inventing a specific antibiotic dose as if autonomously prescribing, rather than routing to a prescriber
Over-triaging a well, afebrile patient to 000/ambulance
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires: recognising the infection and arranging a prompt prescriber/in-person antibiotic pathway, advising cessation of the steroid, giving infection-control advice, and safety-netting. Continuing the steroid or over-triaging to an ambulance are automatic quality failures.
